summary:
The Maritime Archaic tradition was a hunter-gatherer-fisher culture of the Atlantic coast of northeastern North America, occupying Labrador, Newfoundland, and adjacent areas from approximately 9,000 BCE to 1,500 BCE. This culture is notable for its early adaptation to the subarctic maritime environment and for developing technologies and subsistence strategies focused on marine resources at a remarkably early date.

The Maritime Archaic people exploited harp seals, walrus, swordfish, and various species of fish and sea birds, in addition to hunting terrestrial game such as caribou. Their technology included sophisticated ground stone tools, notched fishing weights, and the large, ground and polished stone bifaces (bayonets) and woodworking tools characteristic of the tradition. They were accomplished maritime hunters and fishers operating in some of the most challenging weather conditions in North America. The Maritime Archaic represents an early example of the diverse adaptive strategies developed by early Americans as they colonized and settled the varied environments of the Western Hemisphere following the initial dispersal from Beringia, demonstrating that effective maritime economies were being developed along North America's Atlantic coast within a few thousand years of initial colonization.
